jokes.text: The art teacher is fat, not pregnant.
jokes.setup: Classroom observation or misunderstanding about teacher's appearance
jokes.punchline: Blunt clarification that crushes any romantic notion
jokes.explanation: Brutally efficient opening gag - maximum discomfort in minimal words. The economy is perfect but character integration is weak since we don't know who's speaking.

jokes.text: Taxicab License. What the...?
jokes.setup: Marge discovers something unexpected in Homer's car
jokes.punchline: Homer apparently has a taxi license, revealing his hidden past/schemes
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer character reveal - of course he'd have a random taxi license. Good setup for his later car obsession.

jokes.text: Fine! We'll divide the car into your side and my side. My leg is the barrier.
jokes.punchline: Bart's ridiculous solution using his leg as a physical boundary
jokes.explanation: Solid kid logic - the leg barrier is absurd but exactly what Bart would do. Sets up the cleat payoff.

jokes.text: Mom! / The system works!
jokes.setup: Lisa presumably gets hurt by Bart's leg barrier
jokes.punchline: Bart's triumphant declaration that his stupid system is effective
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart - completely missing the point that hurting his sister isn't success. The economy and character integration are excellent.

jokes.text: It's not funny! Your wearing cleats!
jokes.punchline: Reveals Bart is wearing cleats, explaining the damage
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - of course Bart would be wearing cleats. Explains why the leg barrier is so painful. Well-earned visual gag.

jokes.text: Sorry I'm late... I was in a car crash. Many hurt, but nobody cool was harmed.
jokes.punchline: His sociopathic dismissal of victims based on coolness
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Bart line - perfectly captures his narcissistic worldview where coolness trumps human suffering. Dark but true to character.

jokes.text: Who's the new meat in my seat?
jokes.setup: Bart discovers someone in his desk
jokes.punchline: Prison/gang terminology applied to elementary school seating
jokes.explanation: Good tough-guy posturing from Bart. The alliteration and prison terminology work well for his character.

jokes.text: P.S. That school sucked.
jokes.setup: Reference to being kicked out of P.S. 132
jokes.punchline: 'P.S.' works as both 'Public School' and 'postscript'
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that works on multiple levels. Very economical and structurally sound, though not deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: Hey, Krabappel, your name sounds like 'crab apple.' Did you go sour waiting for someone to pick you? / Pretty much.
jokes.setup: Donny's name-based insult about Mrs. Krabappel
jokes.punchline: Her weary acceptance of the metaphor about her love life
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Krabappel - her deadpan acceptance of the insult reveals her resignation about her romantic failures.

jokes.text: Isn't he awesome? When he grows up, I want to be like me!
jokes.punchline: Mangled version of 'When I grow up, I want to be like him'
jokes.explanation: Good kid logic error that reveals Bart's narcissism even while praising someone else. Character-specific malapropism.

jokes.text: Look, new kid, I'm the head hamster in this habitrail, capisce? / Well, maybe there's a new guinea pig in your cage. Capisce that? / Oh, I capisce. I capisce just fine. / Oh, well, you just keep on capisce-ing.
jokes.setup: Bart's territorial hamster metaphor and Italian tough-guy talk
jokes.punchline: Both kids getting caught up in saying 'capisce' repeatedly
jokes.explanation: Decent escalation of tough-guy posturing devolving into word repetition. The hamster/guinea pig metaphor mixing is appropriately kid-like.

jokes.text: Loa... ner? She's right here. This is the kind of car you see in commercials! How much am I paying for this? - Nothing. - Hm, it seems like a lot.
jokes.setup: Homer learning about free loaner car
jokes.punchline: His suspicion that free seems expensive
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - his brain can't process 'free' as a concept. Perfect character integration and absurdist reasoning.

jokes.text: You people are idiots! / That's the dumb... / What a stu... I mean, that sounds fair.
jokes.setup: Homer calling the mechanics idiots for giving him a free car
jokes.punchline: His quick recovery when he remembers he needs them
jokes.explanation: Good Homer impulse control failure and recovery. Shows his inability to keep his thoughts internal even when it hurts him.

jokes.text: Look at you! Idiots! Driver of a loaner car Much better than a Driver of my normal car...
jokes.setup: Homer singing about his car upgrade
jokes.punchline: His terrible improvised song celebrating being superior to his past self
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer - celebrating minor temporary upgrades with terrible songs. Character integration is perfect.

jokes.text: Whoa! Fancy wheels! What song does the horn play? / None. / Classy.
jokes.punchline: Homer thinking no song is the classy feature
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup from Ned's lower-class assumption and Homer's accidental sophistication. Great economy and structure.

jokes.text: Son, I know when we first met, we didn't really hit it off, but since then I've really come to respect you.
jokes.punchline: Homer talking about 'meeting' his own son
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer parenting - treating his relationship with Bart like a business partnership. Perfectly captures his emotional detachment.

jokes.text: And unlike love, respect can't be bought.
jokes.punchline: Casual admission that love can be bought
jokes.explanation: Dark wisdom from Homer - accidentally revealing his transactional view of relationships while trying to be inspirational.

jokes.text: Now let's watch the air conditioner fight the heater.
jokes.punchline: Homer's idea of quality entertainment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer activity - finding drama in mechanical systems. Shows his child-like fascination with the mundane.

jokes.text: Now let's add the rear window defogger. / Twister! Get down!
jokes.setup: Homer adding more car features to his 'fight'
jokes.punchline: Treating it like a natural disaster requiring shelter
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Homer's mechanical battle fantasy. The weather emergency response adds absurdist flavor.

jokes.text: 'Air Skinner' brings it up. He bounces the ball repeatedly in a dribbling type motion.
jokes.punchline: Overly formal description of basic dribbling
jokes.explanation: Good Skinner voice - his overly formal, bureaucratic way of describing simple actions. The 'Air Skinner' pun is decent.

jokes.text: Great, I fouled out. Now can I go? / Nope, I grant you five more fouls.
jokes.punchline: Skinner's arbitrary extension of punishment
jokes.explanation: Good Skinner power move - changing rules arbitrarily to extend Bart's punishment. Shows his petty authoritarianism.

jokes.text: Students may spend up to ten points per day in up to four of seven categories. Unused points roll over week to week but not month to month. Categories not included: meat, dairy, vegetables...
jokes.setup: Overly complex school lunch plan explanation
jokes.punchline: Excludes all actual food categories
jokes.explanation: Great school bureaucracy satire - creating complex systems that defeat their own purpose. The exclusion of food from lunch is perfect absurdity.

jokes.text: It's not funny; these movements are involuntary.
jokes.setup: Chalmers presumably having some kind of physical reaction
jokes.punchline: His serious clarification that it's medical, not comedic
jokes.explanation: Solid dignity-preservation attempt from Chalmers. The deadpan delivery of medical excuse while presumably flailing is good character work.

jokes.text: This almost makes me want to find out how magnets work.
jokes.punchline: The school genius admitting ignorance about basic physics
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa moment - even the smartest kid doesn't know everything. The casual admission of gaps in knowledge is endearing and realistic.

jokes.text: When I catch the culprit and I will... I'm gonna throw this away... and use this instead!
jokes.punchline: Presumably switching from mild to severe punishment implement
jokes.explanation: Standard escalation gag. Without seeing the objects, it's hard to judge the visual impact. Relies on props for the joke.

jokes.text: Why did you take the rap for me? / I don't know. I like your style.
jokes.punchline: Shallow aesthetic appreciation as deep bond reason
jokes.explanation: Good kid logic - style trumps deeper reasons for friendship. Economical and believable character interaction.

jokes.text: Young man, brace yourself... for some congratulations! We did it! You're in!
jokes.punchline: Surprise celebration instead of punishment
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection structure. The formal 'brace yourself' buildup to congratulations works well. Reveals the spy plot twist effectively.

jokes.text: By law, the most we can give him is a ten-day suspension. / Well, that's long to a kid!
jokes.punchline: Skinner's desperate justification of mild penalty
jokes.explanation: Good Skinner desperation - trying to make minimal punishment seem significant. Shows his powerlessness against Bart.

jokes.text: Uter, Ralph, Doug the hip guidance counselor... he was never the same after Bart got through with him.
jokes.punchline: Adult guidance counselor being psychologically damaged by child
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold - a professional adult counselor being broken by a 10-year-old. Great escalation from kid spies to adult casualty.

jokes.text: Young man, I'm gonna be on you like a numerator on a denominator!
jokes.punchline: Math metaphor for intimidation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner - trying to sound tough using academic terminology. The math metaphor is wonderfully nerdy and character-specific.

jokes.text: I've never seen blue vines before. / They're only sold in Europe. I know a guy.
jokes.punchline: Bart's mysterious international connections for candy
jokes.explanation: Good Bart criminal mystique - treating candy sourcing like international smuggling. The casual 'I know a guy' is perfect kid tough-talk.

jokes.text: You can really taste the blue.
jokes.punchline: Tasting color as flavor
jokes.explanation: Standard artificial flavor joke. The synesthesia of tasting color works but isn't particularly character-specific or original.

jokes.text: We can rig a cootie catcher so you can marry anybody you want. / Whoa! Sherri! I'm in.
jokes.setup: Bart's criminal schemes expanding to playground games
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's immediate enthusiasm for rigged romance
jokes.explanation: Perfect kid logic - treating playground fortune-telling as serious matchmaking. Good Milhouse desperation and specific crush reference.

jokes.text: How is that fun? / Fun's only fun if everyone's having fun.
jokes.punchline: Homer's circular philosophy about inclusive fun
jokes.explanation: Good Homer pseudo-wisdom. The tautological definition of fun sounds profound but is meaningless, which fits his character.

jokes.text: Do you do it with relish? / No, I'm kind of embarrassed about it.
jokes.setup: Homer asking about Marge's condiment saving
jokes.punchline: Marge missing the condiment pun, answering about her emotional state
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay structure. Homer's pun setup and Marge's sincere response about shame creates nice mismatch. Good economy.

jokes.text: What do you mean my car's ready?! Well, you can keep it, 'cause I'm never giving up this one.
jokes.punchline: Rejecting his own repaired car for temporary substitute
jokes.explanation: Good Homer impulsiveness - instant attachment to anything new. Shows his inability to appreciate what he has until he has something else.

jokes.text: That wasn't us, that was the transmission.
jokes.setup: Homer claiming to make love to Marge on phone
jokes.punchline: Revealing the sounds are mechanical, not romantic
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection structure. Sets up romantic expectation then reveals mechanical reality. The deadpan delivery works well.

jokes.text: Skinner's five steps ahead of me. I put a tack in his chair, he's corked his pants! I throw a tomato, he's making salad!
jokes.punchline: Skinner turning every attack into practical advantage
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure. Each example gets more absurd - from cork pants to impromptu salad. Great frustrated Bart voice and clever Skinner defense mechanisms.

jokes.text: Because of your stupid accent? / Nach-nae! Because of its upsetting nature!
jokes.setup: Bart assuming Willie's accent is the communication problem
jokes.punchline: Willie's indignant correction about content vs. delivery
jokes.explanation: Good character interaction. Willie's pride in his communication clarity despite obvious accent barrier is endearing self-delusion.

jokes.text: A child thanked me! I gotta write this in my journal. Dear... diary, Today I've...
jokes.punchline: His formal diary documentation of minor kindness
jokes.explanation: Heartbreaking Willie moment - his social isolation makes simple gratitude diary-worthy. Perfect character vulnerability and formal diary tone.

jokes.text: You're in line for a measle shot. / Okay, now we've made a guide hole for the real needle.
jokes.punchline: Revealing the shot was just prep for more painful procedure
jokes.explanation: Good medical horror escalation. The casual revelation that the painful shot was just a preview works well structurally.

jokes.text: You grew tired of always being in my shadow. / No, I like your shadow. It's nice and cool.
jokes.setup: Bart's dramatic accusation about metaphorical shadow
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's literal interpretation about physical comfort
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse response - completely missing dramatic metaphor for practical appreciation. Excellent character integration and misdirection structure.

jokes.text: Oh, my Gosh. It's an outhouse sent from the future. / Hey, I'm in here. / Be quiet, Robo-potty.
jokes.punchline: Dismissing Milhouse as artificial intelligence
jokes.explanation: Creative Nelson imagination - turning mundane porta-potty into time-travel technology. The 'Robo-potty' dismissal of Milhouse is perfectly mean.

jokes.text: Normally, you can't even buy these. You have to buy the ostrich and wait.
jokes.punchline: Casual discussion of livestock purchase for egg access
jokes.explanation: Great Bart criminal expertise - treating exotic livestock investment as normal prank preparation. The casual 'and wait' is perfect.

jokes.text: Whoa, to think this came out of some animal's butt. / Beautiful, in its way.
jokes.punchline: Bart's philosophical acceptance of gross reality
jokes.explanation: Standard gross-out followed by mock-profound acceptance. Good character contrast between Milhouse's crudeness and Bart's fake sophistication.

jokes.text: I never dreamed an American car designed in Germany, assembled in Mexico from parts made in Canada could be so amazing.
jokes.punchline: Describing completely non-American 'American' car
jokes.explanation: Brilliant globalization satire. The complete international supply chain for 'American' manufacturing is both accurate and absurd. Well-earned economic critique.

jokes.text: Dad? / Sorry, Maggie.
jokes.setup: Homer's comparison of car to beloved child
jokes.punchline: Apologizing to actual child for the comparison
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer parenting moment - realizing he's placed material object above actual child. Excellent economy and character truth.

jokes.text: Ah, get back to your knitting, Seymour. / I will, but it's because I want to.
jokes.punchline: Skinner's dignified assertion of hobby autonomy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Skinner response - refusing to be shamed for domestic hobbies while maintaining dignity. Excellent character consistency and structure.

jokes.text: Look, you don't start out wanting to be a rat. Sometimes it's your only option.
jokes.punchline: Treating elementary school spying like organized crime necessity
jokes.explanation: Good crime drama pastiche applied to school context. Donny's serious justification of playground betrayal works well.

jokes.text: Too cute, too tall, too Siamese, too Siamese.
jokes.punchline: Rejecting twins for being too obviously connected
jokes.explanation: Good bureaucratic selection process absurdity. The repeated 'too Siamese' for twins is economical and mildly clever.

jokes.text: You see, the rat who told you about my rat was also a rat. It was rats within rats... which was also me dinner last night.
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of eating rats
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from metaphorical to literal rats. Willie's casual dietary revelation maintains his lower-class characterization well.

jokes.text: He gave me something you never could... an elementary school diploma.
jokes.punchline: Adult groundskeeper getting elementary school credential
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Willie character moment - his educational insecurity makes elementary diploma meaningful. Perfect mix of pathetic and heartwarming.

jokes.text: The rat symbolizes obviousness.
jokes.punchline: Stating the obvious symbolism obviously
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa ending - intellectualizing the episode's theme while being completely obvious about it. Good meta-commentary structure.
